story:
Jaxon Crenshaw launches late and angry, a marshal in a single-seat aeropod skimming over the greened-over skeleton of old roads. Wild birds are museum pieces now; traffic rules the sky. His cheek tic keeps time with each delay. He’s headed to a fading rural prison to meet Anne “Annie” Landon—once a teenage mass shooter made infamous by a blue BMW spattered black with dried blood and a mugshot whose eyes look shocked at themselves. Along the river, the last peeling billboards plead: Free Annie. No Child Is Born BAD.

The governor sent him. Mass incarceration is over; the prison will soon be demolished and turned into a raptor sanctuary. The city-state’s Reform Project has released people with higher body counts after treatment. They can’t force surgery anymore—law forbids it—but if Annie attacks, self-defense is clear. Dress the part, the governor said. Jaxon does.

The guards let him keep his gun and review the rules. The partition between them is one-way glass backed by a permeable field. Use the pass-through if you must; objects may go from your side into hers, not the reverse. Do not drop the field. If you drop it, it becomes two-way. Keep the weapon hidden. Breathe. Jaxon counts to cage his temper.

Annie is old now. The dramatic white streak that once made her iconic has faded into gray. Her face is lined—not theatrically, but honestly, by time. Her room is stripped of tools yet strangely homey: a quilt sewn from old orange jumpsuits, and walls covered with pencil portraits of one man—Bill Rodriguez—drawn dozens of times. His eyes are tender. His mouth is missing.

That’s Bill, she says. He visits me the most. I draw him at the moment before I shot—when, behind the mask, he didn’t have a face anymore.

“Why no mouth?” Jaxon asks, though he already knows.

Masks. During the 2020 pandemic, Blue cities masked first; Red states later, and more bitterly. A Safeway was targeted online as a militant mask enforcer. Annie went there with her father’s gun and shot fifty-eight people. She never saw the lower half of Bill’s face. Or his granddaughter’s.

Jaxon says he didn’t come to recite the body count. He wants to know how she feels now.

Annie’s answer is layered, bitter, self-aware. Was her trial fair? She laughs. She was homeschooled into a bubble, her social studies a steady drip of partisan TV. Her father drank. Her mother prayed and apologized. The house bent everything crooked. By late adolescence she was all voltage: a tiger of rage with nowhere honest to go.

“Rage isn’t special,” she says. “You have it too.”
He denies it. She watches the tiny tell. He thinks of his ex-wife, his kids, the relocation he blames on a solar flare rather than his outbursts, the pills and mantras that keep him level.

Jaxon pitches freedom: monitored housing, counseling—if she wants it, even voluntary surgery to stabilize her chemistry.

Annie studies the quilt, the drawings—her second and only real home. “Outside is worse,” she says. “I’m not mended. I shouldn’t get out. Don’t make me the test case for your better world and its delays.”

He tells her the prison will fall to make room for a raptor sanctuary—eagles pushed out by drones and pods.
“Trading one predator for another,” she says, almost smiling. Then she invokes an old theory: every eighty years, violence crests; the 2020s were one such wave. She wasn’t the cause so much as a consequence—still responsible, never excused.

“Why draw Bill and not Alivia?” Jaxon asks. His granddaughter, Alivia, had been seven.

“The girl’s mask hid more,” Annie says. “Afterward… she had no face at all.” Tears rise. “Please—may I have a tissue?”

Reflex overrides training. Jaxon plucks a tissue from the dispenser and thumbs the pass-through. The tissue slides safely into her side, caught by the one-way field. But when it snags at the lip of the slot, he curses, leans in, and hits the manual override instead of the release. The permeable field drops for a beat.

Annie moves—quick, practiced, propelled not by surprise but by inevitability—past the tissue. Her arm knifes through the opening toward the holster Jaxon was told to keep hidden but hasn’t; his jacket has ridden up.

Her expression isn’t triumph. It’s reproach, and something like grief:

I told you who I was. Now look what you made me do.
